utilityName: City of Grand Junction Backflow Prevention Program
testingFrequency: At installation, annually, and when moved or repaired
dueBasis: Grand Junction says backflow preventers are required under Colorado drinking-water regulations and city resolution, and that PVBAs must be tested at installation, annually, and when moved or repaired.
coveredPropertyTypes: Home sprinkler systems tied to domestic water
coveredDeviceTypes: Home and business sprinkler backflow preventers
penalties: Grand Junction frames the program as mandatory under state regulations and city resolution. The practical risk is leaving home, business, irrigation, or fire-sprinkler systems without required backflow protection.
sourceExcerpt: Grand Junction is a useful Colorado city because it publishes clear hazard examples, explicit sprinkler and fire-sprinkler triggers, and annual-plus-moved-or-repaired testing rules on the device pages.
verdictSummary: Grand Junction is a useful Colorado city because it turns sprinkler, fire-sprinkler, and chemical-use backflow rules into straightforward local guidance.
whoIsAffected: Grand Junction homes, businesses, sprinkler systems, fire-sprinkler systems, and other services that could contaminate the public water supply if backflow protection is missing.
residentialNotes: Grand Junction is unusually good for residential pages because the city specifically calls out home sprinkler systems tied into domestic water.
Winterization and homeowner safety language give the page useful non-commercial depth.
commercialNotes: Commercial value is solid because the city names business sprinklers, fire sprinklers, and chemical-use facilities directly.
This is a good city for irrigation and sprinkler-intent landing pages.

annualTesting.summary: Grand Junction says PVBAs must be tested at installation, annually, and when moved or repaired.
highlights: Annual testing is explicit on the design-requirements page.
The city ties the rule to Colorado drinking-water regulations and a city resolution.
Home and business sprinkler systems are both named publicly.
workflowSteps: Confirm the assembly type and whether it serves home, business, or irrigation use.
Complete the annual or post-repair test.
Keep the device in working condition so contaminated water cannot re-enter the potable system.

irrigation.summary: Grand Junction is especially useful for irrigation because the city publishes a dedicated PVBA page for sprinkler systems tied into the domestic water supply.
highlights: Only one PVB is required to serve the whole system when installed correctly.
PVBAs must sit at least 12 inches above the highest point they serve.
No chemicals or fertilizers may be introduced into a system equipped with a PVBA.
workflowSteps: Confirm the sprinkler or irrigation system is tied into domestic water.
Install the PVBA at the required height and layout.
Keep annual, moved, and repaired-device tests current.

fireLine.summary: Grand Junction openly says fire sprinkler systems need backflow preventers, which makes fire-side compliance a real local page.
highlights: Fire sprinkler systems are named directly in the FAQ.
Annual testing still applies once the device is in service.
The city treats fire protection as part of the same drinking-water safety problem.
workflowSteps: Confirm the device is serving a fire sprinkler system.
Use the correct backflow assembly class for the protected service.
Keep the annual and post-repair testing current.
Identify whether the system is sprinkler, irrigation, fire sprinkler, or another protected service.
Install or verify the correct backflow device.
Test the device at installation and annually.
Retest after moves or repairs so the record stays current.
failureHighlights: Grand Junction names sprinkler and fire-sprinkler systems directly.
The device pages include specific installation and chemical-use rules.
Annual and post-repair testing are both explicit.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but Grand Junction adds very clear sprinkler-specific rules that narrow the work scope.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest costs rise where sprinkler layouts, height clearances, or chemical-injection risks complicate the job.
costBand.pricingNotes: The main local value is clear device-specific rules for sprinkler-heavy properties.
